title: Bourse falls again ahead of looming RBA rates call
full_text: Other Editions 05 May 2026 MarketWatch Bourse falls again ahead of looming RBA rates call The Australian Ike Morris, Equities 5 May 2026 619 words English AUSTLN 19 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Fears of further interest rate pain and unfavourable company announcements continued the sell-off of the Australian sharemarket on Monday. The ASX 200 closed lower, dropping 32.7 points, or 0.38 per cent, to 8697.1, while the broader All Ordinaries dropped 30.9 points, or 0.35 per cent, to 8923.8. The big four banks had a mixed day of trading. NAB shares fell by 1.58 per cent or 62c to $39.20 after it posted a drop in half-year profits, while Commonwealth Bank dropped 0.48 per cent or 82c to $172.21. ANZ rose 67c, or 1.9 per cent, to $36.29, and Westpac recorded a 0.13 per cent increase to finish the day 5c higher at $38.50. Gold prices finished weaker by $US23.41 to $US4613.62 per ounce, while the price of Brent crude oil trended down by 0.87 points, or 0.8 per cent, to trade at $US107.30 per barrel. The Australian dollar was buying US72.02c – its highest level against the greenback in four years. Most ASX sectors finished trading in the red. Consumer staples was down by 2.58 per cent. Endeavour Group was weaker by 3.8 per cent, or 12c, to finish at $3.29 after mentioning plans in its quarterly update to cut costs by $100m over the 2027 financial year. Coles Group trended down by 3.93 per cent or 90c to $22.02, while Bega Cheese fell 20c, or 3.58 per cent to $5.38. Utilities also finished the day lower, with AGL falling by 3.1 per cent or 30c to $9.39. Information technology recorded a 1.03 per cent increase. Life360 was up 6.15 per cent or $1.23 at $21.23, while Xero Limited shares rose by 2.92 per cent, finishing $2.35 stronger at $82.92. Investors will be looking closely at the Reserve Bank’s interest rates decision on Tuesday, as another rate hike is widely tipped due to the global energy shocks caused by the ongoing Middle East war. BetaShares economist David Bassanese said while the decision would likely be “disappointing” for mortgage holders, the sharemarket may not be significantly affected over the days. “Given it’s expected, the decision shouldn’t have a big effect on the market (on Tuesday),” he said. “The tone of the (RBA’s) statement that accompanies the decision will be probably just as important as the decision itself ... “The market may be relieved if they raise rates but then signal that they’ll (the RBA) be pausing (them) for some time. “The RBA needs to slow the economy so that the hit to energy inflation doesn’t become embedded through a wage price setting.” In company news, footwear retailer Accent’s share price plunged 12.9 per cent, or 8c, to 54c, after the company said it was dealing with an ASIC investigation on market trading by CEO Daniel Agostinelli, and that it slashed pre-tax earnings well below market expectations to between $79.5m and $84.5m for FY26. Viva Energy shares trended down by 3.2 per cent, or 8c, to $2.42 after it announced repairs to its Geelong oil refinery following a major fire last month were expected to be completed by the end of June. Meanwhile, infant formula company A2 Milk plunged 9.9 per cent, or 72c to $6.55, following an announcement that it was recalling thousands of baby formula units shipped to the US after discovering a toxin that could cause serious illness to children. summary: Older Australians will face an increase in private health insurance costs of over $1600 starting April next year due to the Albanese government's decision to eliminate higher rebates for those over 65. This change is part of broader reforms impacting healthcare funding. The adjustment may lead to increased financial pressure on seniors and their families.
publication:
Seniors to cop a $1600 health cover hike
Daily Telegraph05 May 2026279 wordsJulie Cross

Older Australians are facing private health insurance price hikes of more than $1600 from April next year, under the Albanese government’s plan to scrap higher rebates for over-65s.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Private health insurance costs for seniors to rise by over $1600
Government to scrap higher rebates for over-65s
Potential financial impact on older Australians and their families
relevance: This news is relevant as it highlights changes in health insurance policy that could affect financial planning and risk assessment for actuaries and financial services professionals working with older clients.
